skill.name: subagent-driven-development
skill.description: Coordinate independent implementation tasks with fresh subagents, spec review, and code quality review in one session.
skill.summary: Run implementation plans through task-specific subagents with structured review gates.
skill.author: obra

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown fences, Git SHA placeholders, task descriptions, and workflow examples rather than executable code. The skill can guide coding subagents to read files, edit code, run tests, and commit work, so it has normal coding-workflow risk but no evidence of malicious intent, data exfiltration, or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misread as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences and inline file references used to document prompt templates. No Ruby code, shell interpolation, or executable command is present at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matching characters delimit Markdown blocks and inline paths. I found no executable Ruby, command substitution, or instruction to run hidden shell commands.

low_findings.title: Git SHA and Description Text Misread as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported weak cryptography matches occur in front matter descriptions, task description placeholders, and Git commit SHA placeholders. These references do not request MD5, SHA-1 hashing, password storage, or cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only SHA references are Git revision placeholders used by a code review workflow. There is no cryptographic API use or security-sensitive hash handling.

low_findings.title: Review Checklist Terms Misread as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported system and network reconnaissance lines are checklist prompts about implementation completeness, overbuilding, existing patterns, and code inspection. No host discovery, network scanning, service enumeration, or external endpoint probing is requested.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text is a software review rubric. It asks the agent to inspect code and requirements, not systems or networks.

low_findings.title: Home Configuration Path Is a Workflow Example
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hidden home directory reference is an example answer about where a hook should be installed. It does not instruct the skill to read secrets, traverse home directories, or access hidden files without user direction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path appears inside a sample conversation and is explicitly provided by the user in that example. The skill does not contain code that accesses the path.

low_findings.title: Coding Subagent Workflow Requires Normal Repository Access
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill intentionally directs implementer and reviewer subagents to work from a directory, read code, run verification, commit changes, and inspect Git SHAs. This is expected for a coding workflow but should be visible to users because it can modify repository state.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow plainly asks subagents to implement, test, self-review, commit, and inspect code. This is legitimate behavior for the skill, but it is a real capability with filesystem and command side effects.

content.value_statement: Large implementation plans often lose quality when one agent carries too much context. This skill splits work into focused subagent tasks with spec and code review gates.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use same-session subagent execution for independent tasks.
Provides an implementer prompt template for task-scoped coding work.
Provides a spec compliance reviewer prompt for requirement-by-requirement checks.
Provides a code quality reviewer prompt that runs after spec approval.
Defines review loops that continue until issues are fixed and rechecked.
Documents red flags for skipping reviews, overbuilding, or moving on too early.
limitations: It is a workflow guide, not an automated task scheduler.
It assumes the user already has a clear implementation plan.
It works best when tasks are mostly independent.
It can increase token and tool usage because each task receives multiple reviews.

use_cases.title: Execute Feature Plans
use_cases.description: Break a feature plan into task prompts, assign each task to a fresh implementer, and review every result.

use_cases.title: Maintain Review Discipline
use_cases.description: Add spec compliance and quality review checkpoints before accepting implementation work.

use_cases.title: Reduce Context Drift
use_cases.description: Keep long coding sessions organized by giving each subagent only the task and context it needs.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin with one independent task from an existing plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use subagent-driven development for this task. Extract the full task text, add the required context, and dispatch one implementer subagent.

prompt_templates.title: Run the Review Loop
prompt_templates.scenario: Check a completed task before marking it done.
prompt_templates.prompt: After the implementer reports back, dispatch the spec compliance reviewer. If it passes, dispatch the code quality reviewer. Send any findings back for fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Execute a Multi-Task Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Work through several mostly independent tasks in one session.
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the implementation plan once, create a task list, and execute each task with a fresh implementer plus both review stages.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Review Failures
prompt_templates.scenario: A reviewer found missing requirements or quality issues.
prompt_templates.prompt: Send the reviewer findings back to the same implementer subagent for correction, then rerun the relevant review until it passes.

output_examples.input: Execute Task 1 from a feature plan.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares the task text, adds context, dispatches an implementer, records test results, and starts review.

output_examples.input: The spec reviewer reports a missing progress requirement.
output: The spec reviewer checks the fix again before quality review starts.

output_examples.input: All tasks in the plan are complete.
output_examples.output: The assistant requests a final review of the full implementation and then moves to branch finishing steps.

best_practices: Use it only after a plan exists and task boundaries are clear.
Give each subagent complete task text and only the context it needs.
Run spec compliance review before code quality review for every task.
anti_patterns: Do not use it for tightly coupled tasks that require constant shared context.
Do not skip review loops when a reviewer finds issues.
Do not ask implementer subagents to rediscover the plan from files when you can provide the task text.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps execute implementation plans by assigning each task to a fresh subagent and reviewing the result in stages.

faq.question: Does it write code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow instructions and prompt templates for agents that perform coding work.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when you have an implementation plan with mostly independent tasks and want to stay in one session.

faq.question: Why are there two review stages?
faq.answer: Spec review checks whether the requested work was built. Code quality review checks maintainability, tests, and design.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code style coding sessions.

faq.question: What should I watch for?
faq.answer: Watch for skipped reviews, unclear task context, parallel implementation conflicts, and accepted work with unresolved findings.
